children.value: This page contains a working draft of
Interfaces for Personal Identity Verification: Part 1 –
Card Application Namespace, Data Model, and Representation
, as it is being updated to support post-quantum cryptography. The current, final version of this publication,

201 defines the requirements and characteristics of government-wide interoperable identity credentials. It specifies that these identity credentials must be stored on a smart card and that additional common identity credentials, known as derived
credentials, may be issued by a federal department or agency and used when a
Card is not practical. This document contains the technical specifications to interface with the smart card to retrieve and use
identity credentials. The specifications reflect the design goals of interoperability and
Card functions. The goals are addressed by specifying a

data model, card edge interface, and application programming interface. Moreover, this document enumerates requirements for the options and branches in international integrated circuit card standards. The specifications go further by constraining interpretations of the normative standards to ease implementation, facilitate interoperability, and ensure performance in a manner tailored for
The authors --- Hildegard Ferraiolo, Ketan Mehta, Salvatore Francomacaro, and Ramaswamy Chandramouli of

children.title: National Institute of Standards and Technology

children.value: and Sarbari Gupta of Electrosoft Services, Inc. --- gratefully acknowledge the contributions of David Cooper, James Dray, William MacGregor, Scott Guthery, Teresa Schwarzhoff, and Jason Mohler, who co-authored prior versions of this three-part publication. The authors also gratefully acknowledge and appreciate the many contributions from the public and private sectors whose thoughtful and constructive comments improved the quality and usefulness of this publication.

-12) called for the adoption of a common identification standard to govern the interoperable use of identity credentials to allow physical and logical access to federally controlled facilities and information systems. In response, Federal Information Processing Standard (

, was developed to define reliable, government-wide identity credentials for use in applications such as access to federally controlled facilities and information systems.

The following two data elements are mandatory if the cardholder has a government-issued email account at the time of credential issuance. These two data elements, when implemented,
LL conform to the specifications provided in this document.
The X.509 Certificate for Digital Signature and its associated private key, as defined in
201, support the use of digital signatures for the purpose of document signing. The digital signature private key and its corresponding certificate are only available over the contact interface or
. The read access control rule for the X.509 Certificate for Digital Signing is “Always,” meaning that the certificate CAN be read without access control restrictions. The
Always” access rule. In other words, the
data must be submitted and verified every time immediately before a
operation. This ensures cardholder participation every time the private key is used for digital signature generation.
The X.509 Certificate for Key Management and its associated private key, as defined in
201, support the use of encryption for the purpose of confidentiality. The key management private key and its corresponding certificate are only available over the contact interface or

. This key pair MAY be escrowed by the issuer for key recovery purposes. The read access control rule for the X.509 certificate is “Always,” meaning that the certificate CAN be read without access control restrictions. The
” access rule. In other words, once the
data is submitted and verified, subsequent
operations CAN be performed without requiring the
data again. This enables multiple private key operations without additional cardholder consent.

201 mandatory information printed on the card is duplicated on the chip in that data object. The printed information data object

LL NOT be modified post-issuance. The Security Object enforces integrity of this information according to the issuer. This provides specific protection that the card information must match the printed information, mitigating alteration risks on the printed media.

Retired key management keys may include a mix of
, and ML-KEM keys. The Key History mechanism (and off-card certificate file) will need to accommodate certificates with different algorithm types and varying sizes.
Up to 20 retired key management private keys MAY be stored in the
Card Application. The Key History object provides information about the retired key management private keys that are present within the

Retired key management private keys are private keys that correspond to X.509 Certificates for Key Management that have expired, have been revoked, or have otherwise been superseded. The Key History object
Card Application contains any retired key management private keys but MAY be present even if no such keys are present in the
Card Application. For each retired key management private key in the
Card Application, the corresponding certificate MAY either be present within the
Card Application or MAY only be available from an online repository.

data again. This enables multiple private key operations without additional cardholder consent.

Data Object Containers and Associated Access Rules and Interface Modes

children.label: Data Object Containers and Associated Access Rules and Interface Modes

children.value: defines a high-level view of the data model. Each on-card storage container is labeled as mandatory (M), optional (O), or conditional (C). The conditional data objects are the digital signature key and the key management key, which are mandatory if the cardholder has a government-issued email account at the time of credential issuance. This data model is designed to enable and support dual interface cards. For dual chip implementations for any container that can be accessed over both the contact interface and the contactless interface (including the virtual contact interface), the data object
LL be copied into the corresponding containers on both chips.
New containers will be needed for PQC-signed data objects (e.g.,
, Security Object) and PQC certificates, particularly if both classical and PQC versions need to coexist during the transition period.

The content of a data object is the sequence of bytes that are said to be contained in or to be the value of the data object. The number of bytes in this byte sequence is referred to as the length of the data content as well as the size of the data object. The first byte in the sequence is regarded as being at byte position or offset zero in the content of the data object.

This section describes the data types used in the
800-73-5, Part 2). Unless otherwise indicated, the representation
LL be the same on both interfaces.

The data types are defined in Part 1 rather than in Parts 2 and 3 in order to achieve smart card platform independence from Part 1. Thus, non-government smart card programs can readily adopt the interface specifications in Parts 2 and 3 while customizing Part 1 to their own data model, data types, and namespaces.
A key reference is a 1-byte reference data identifier that specifies a cryptographic key or
, define the key reference values that
. For example, the key reference values are used in a cryptographic protocol, such as an authentication or a signing protocol. Key references are only assigned to private and secret (symmetric) keys,
, and the pairing code. All other
Card Application key reference values are reserved for future use.

Card supports the virtual contact interface, then it
LL implement support for the pairing code. If implemented, the pairing code
LL consist of eight decimal digits, and it
LL be generated at random by the
Card Issuer. The results of each random pairing code generation
LL be loaded onto -- at most -- one
Card and CANNOT be changed by the cardholder. The pairing code value for a
LL be stored in the Pairing Code Reference Data Container (see
) on the card and MAY be printed on the back of the card in an agency-specific text area (i.e., Zones 9B or 10B).
Card Issuers MAY choose to provide the pairing code value to the cardholder in another manner, such as printing it on a slip of paper rather than printing it on the back of the card.
, there are no restrictions on the caching of the pairing code by client applications. It is recommended that a client application that needs to communicate with a

Card over its virtual contact interface obtain the card’s pairing code during a registration step by asking the cardholder to enter the value or by reading it from the card over the contact interface from the Pairing Code Reference Data Container and then cache the pairing code until the card expires.

The client application MAY then connect to the card and establish a virtual contact interface with it whenever the card is within read-range of the client application’s contactless card reader without needing to prompt the cardholder.

. These identifiers serve as inputs to the

800-78-5, Tables 9 and 10, which reflect support for higher strength keys for
cards and supporting systems, where applicable.
All other cryptographic mechanism identifier values are reserved for future use.

800-73-5 Part 2, all non-card management operations that are allowed over the contact interface MAY be carried out over the contactless interface if the
security condition is satisfied. Support for the
security condition supports two different configurations for the establishment of the

protects the previously contact-restricted X.509 certificates from skimming

Card when in close proximity when the card is outside of its protective sleeve.
A DAA’s decision to approve the issuance of

LL be based on a risk assessment that weighs the perceived benefit against the risk of unauthorized disclosure of cardholder data exposing previously contact-restricted X.509 certificates to skimming. The previously contact-restricted X.509 certificates include information about the cardholder, such as name and email address. Compensating controls
LL be captured in the appropriate system security plan.

children.value: authentication mechanisms and application scenarios are described in this section to provide guidelines on the usage and behavior supported by the
authentication as “the process of establishing confidence in the identity of the cardholder presenting a
National Institute of Standards and Technology, 2022
. The fundamental goal of using the

Card is to authenticate the identity of the cardholder to a system or person that is controlling access to a protected resource or facility. This end goal MAY be reached by various combinations of one or more of the validation steps described below:
Card Validation (CardV) -- This is the process of verifying that a
Card is authentic (i.e., not a counterfeit card). Card validation mechanisms include:
Visual inspection of the tamper-proofing and tamper-resistant features of the
Use of cryptographic challenge-response schemes with symmetric keys
Use of asymmetric authentication schemes to validate private keys embedded within the
Credential Validation (CredV) -- This is the process of verifying the various types of credentials (e.g., visual credentials, biometrics, and certificates) held by the
Checking the revocation status of the credentials on the
(e.g., the photo, the printed name, rank).
Cardholder Validation (HolderV) -- This is the process of establishing that the

Card is in the possession of the individual to whom the card was issued. Classically, identity authentication is achieved using one or more of these factors: a) something you have, b) something you know, and c) something you are. The assurance of the authentication process increases with the number of factors used. In the case of the
Card, these three factors translate as follows: a) something you have -- possession of a
Card, b) something you know -- knowledge of the
, and c) something you are -- the live fingerprint, facial image, or iris image samples provided by the cardholder. Thus, mechanisms for
Matching the live fingerprint, facial image, or iris image samples provided by the cardholder with the biometric information embedded within the
Matching the visual characteristics of the cardholder with the photo on the
This section describes the activities and interactions involved in interoperable usage and authentication of the

Card. The authentication mechanisms represent how a relying party will authenticate the cardholder (regardless of which agency issued the card) in order to provide access to its systems or facilities. These activities and interactions are represented in functional authentication mechanism diagrams. These diagrams are not intended to provide syntactical commands or

authentication mechanisms described in this section can be broken into a sequence of one or more validation steps where Card, Credential, and Cardholder validation is performed. In the illustrations, the validation steps are marked as CardV, CredV, and HolderV to signify Card, Credential, and Cardholder validation, respectively.
Depending on the assurance provided by the actual sequence of validation steps in a given
authentication mechanism, relying parties can make appropriate decisions for granting access to protected resources based on a risk analysis.

For example, a relying party performing the authentication mechanism described in
CAN expect to perform a challenge and response cryptographic authentication with a 3072-bit or a 2048-bit
This appendix describes recommended procedures for key size and algorithm discovery (

algorithm ID discovery) to facilitate cryptographic authentication initiated by the relying party to make appropriate decisions for granting access to logical networks and systems as well as physical access control systems. The discovery procedure is defined in terms of asymmetric and symmetric cryptographic authentication.
Algorithm Identifier Discovery for Asymmetric Cryptographic Authentication
As illustrated in the authentication mechanisms in
, an asymmetric cryptographic authentication involves issuing a challenge (request to sign a nonce) to the
Card. The relying party issuing the command provides the nonce to be signed, the key reference, and the
algorithm identifier as parameters of the command. The nonce is random data generated by the relying party, and the key reference is known. In contrast, the
algorithm identifier is unknown to the relying party and needs to be identified in order to issue the challenge command. The

algorithm identifier CAN be derived from the previous steps of the authentication mechanism. Prior to issuing the challenge command, the relying party retrieved and parsed the X.509 certificate from the card to validate the certificate and extract the public key for the pending verification of the signed nonce once returned from the card. The
algorithm identifier CAN be identified during the parsing of the X.509 certificate in two steps:
The X.509 certificate stores the public key in the
data structure has an algorithm field, which includes an
that identifies the public key’s algorithm (
If the algorithm type determined in Step 1 is
, then the key size is determined by the elliptic curve on which the key has been generated, which is P-256 or P-384 for all elliptic curve
Authentication keys and Card Authentication keys.
If the algorithm type determined in Step 1 is
, then the key size is determined by the public key’s modulus. The public key appears in the subjectPublicKey field of
and is encoded as a sequence that includes both the key’s modulus and public exponent.
As a final step, the discovered X.509 algorithm
and key size are mapped to the
algorithm identifiers, as defined in Table 9 of
800-78. The relying party then proceeds to issue the
Algorithm Identifier Discovery for Symmetric Cryptographic Authentication
In the absence of an X.509 certificate, as is the case with symmetric cryptography, the
algorithm identifier discovery mechanism has to rely on a lookup table that resides on the local system. The table maps a unique card identifier and key reference (inputs) to an associated
algorithm identifier (output). The unique identifier supplied by the card MAY be the Agency Code || System Code || Credential Number of the

The symmetric Card Authentication key is optional to implement, and a relying party has no prior knowledge of the key’s existence. The following routine discovers the Card Authentication key’s native implementation:
Agency Code || System code || Credential Number
algorithm identifier from the local lookup table. If no algorithm identifier is returned, authentication CANNOT be performed using the optional symmetric Card Authentication key, either because the
Card does not implement the key or the local system CANNOT authenticate the response from the card.
Algorithm Identifier Discovery for Secure Messaging

This document uses the following typographical conventions in text:
represent normative requirements. When these same terms are not in
, the term does not represent a normative requirement.
indicate requirements to be strictly followed in order to conform to the publication and from which no deviation is permitted.

indicate that among several possibilities, one is recommended as particularly suitable without mentioning or excluding others, that a certain course of action is preferred but not necessarily required, or that --- in the negative form --- a certain possibility or course of action is discouraged but not prohibited.
indicate a course of action that is permissible within the limits of the publication.
indicate a material, physical, or causal possibility or capability or -- in the negative -- the absence of that possibility or capability.

, in which case both keys would share the same key reference and access control rules. However, the use of the symmetric card authentication key has been deprecated in
201-3 and may be removed in a future version of the standard.
The sole use of the pairing code is the establishment of a
. Its use over the contact interface serves no purpose.

While printing the value of the pairing code on the back of the card provides maximum convenience for use by the cardholder and avoids any risk that the cardholder will forget the pairing code, it may create a risk that an attacker could obtain the value of the pairing code by surreptitiously reading it from the back of the card. Departments and agencies will need to make a risk-based decision when determining the method by
, the pairing code does not need to be submitted if the Bit 3 of the first byte of the
Usage Policy is set to one.

Skimming is when data is surreptitiously obtained from a contactless card using a hidden reader that powers, commands, and reads from the card within the maximum read distance (reported as about 25 cm with
800-18r1, Guide for Developing Security Plans for Federal Information Systems.
The values in this column denote the guaranteed minimum capacities of the on-card storage containers in bytes. Cards with larger containers may be produced and determined conformant.
GZIP formats are specified in RFC 1951 cand RFC 1952.
Use of the photo on the
Card for visual authentication has been deprecated in
201-3 and may be removed from a future edition of the standard.
800-78 lists the various algorithms and key sizes that may be used for each
